title: Mourning Rage: The Year After
dek: *One year after a significant loss, many people discover a surprising companion: fury. Not the clean sadness they expected, but a hot, restless anger that lives in the chest and shoulders. This is mourning rage in its second act, and it asks different things of you than early grief did. Here's what happens when you stop trying to be reasonable about it.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. The second year of grief often holds more rage than the first because shock has worn off and reality settles in. Your body stores this anger in fascia, jaw tension, and shallow breathing patterns that require somatic attention, not just talk. Learning to move with mourning rage instead of against it transforms both your nervous system and your relationship to loss.

What if the anger you've been calling "healing setbacks" is actually the grief finally telling the truth? The first year after loss often moves in a fog. Shock absorbs the sharpest edges. People send casseroles and cards. The world makes space, however briefly, for your disorientation. But the second year? The second year is when everyone else has moved on and your body is just beginning to register what actually happened. The rage that surfaces now isn't new. It was there all along, coiled beneath the numbness, waiting for you to have enough ground beneath your feet to feel it without shattering. In my own practice, I've watched this pattern repeat: the client who was "doing so well" at month eight suddenly can't sleep at month fourteen. The friend who seemed to accept their mother's death with grace now snaps at small inconveniences. The widower who returned to work cleanly now fantasizes about throwing his computer through a window. This isn't regression. This is the body finally feeling safe enough to discharge what it couldn't metabolize in real time. ## The Body Keeps the Fury Score Peter Levine's work on trauma and the nervous system offers crucial insight here. He observed that incomplete defensive responses, things the body wanted to do but couldn't, like fight or flee, get stored as tension patterns. In grief, we rarely get to complete our instinctive responses. You can't fight death. You can't run from loss. You can't push away the absence. So the body holds the impulse, and it calculates the cost in muscle tension, shallow breathing, and a kind of background static that colors everything. Your jaw might be the first place you notice. A chronic clench that worsens when you're trying to fall asleep. Or your shoulders, riding up toward your ears in a perpetual brace against the next blow. Some people feel it in their fists, a constant low-grade urge to punch something, even when they're sitting quietly at their desk. This isn't metaphorical. The fascia, the connective tissue that wraps every muscle and organ, literally thickens and binds when we hold defensive impulses for months on end. The CDC's guidance on stress and the body acknowledges that prolonged grief activates the same physiological pathways as chronic stress: elevated cortisol, disrupted sleep architecture, inflammation markers that don't resolve. What they don't always emphasize is that anger, specifically, changes your breathing pattern. When you're chronically furious but can't express it, you breathe high and shallow, using only the top third of your lungs. This keeps you in a state of partial activation, ready to respond to a threat that never fully arrives or departs. The second year is when this holding pattern starts to hurt. Your back goes out for no clear reason. You get tension headaches three times a week. Your digestion becomes erratic. These aren't separate problems. They're your body asking you to complete something that got interrupted. ## What Mourning Rage Actually Wants Here's the thing most grief literature won't tell you: your anger doesn't want you to be calm. It doesn't want perspective or reframing or a gratitude practice. It wants acknowledgment. It wants movement. It wants you to stop being so damn reasonable about something unreasonable. Robert Augustus Masters writes about anger as a form of fierce caring, a boundary-setting energy that says "this matters." In grief, that often translates to: this person mattered, this loss is not okay, the world continuing as if nothing happened is not okay, my being expected to function normally is not okay. The rage is loyal. It refuses to minimize. It won't let you skip over the enormity of what you've lost just to make other people comfortable. Over the years I've seen people try to logic their way out of mourning rage. They make lists of things to be grateful for. They remind themselves that anger is "toxic" or "low vibration." They read books about acceptance and try to force themselves there before the anger has had its say. This almost always backfires. The rage doesn't disappear when you shame it. It goes underground and starts running the show from there, sabotaging relationships, making you irritable and critical, or turning inward into depression. What mourning rage actually wants is simpler and harder: it wants you to let it move through you. Not act it out on people who don't deserve it, but let it have its physical expression in ways that complete the defensive cycle your body started a year ago. ## A Five-Minute Practice for Rage That Has Nowhere to Go Find a private space. Stand with your feet hip-width apart. Take three breaths deep enough that your belly moves. Now make fists. Tight, committed fists. As you exhale, push your fists down toward the floor like you're pushing through thick mud. Make sound if you can, any sound, even just a hard exhale through your teeth. Do this ten times, pushing down with everything you've got. Then switch: pull your fists up toward your chest, elbows bent, like you're pulling yourself up a rope. Again, ten times, with sound on the exhale. Shake out your hands. Notice what's different. You might feel heat in your arms, or a slight tremor, or a surprising urge to cry. All of this is discharge. This is your nervous system completing a cycle it's been stuck in. This isn't about "getting the anger out" as if it's something separate from you. It's about giving your body the movement it's been asking for so it can stop holding the pattern in your tissue. ## The Herbs That Hold Space for Fury Some plants have an affinity for the specific kind of heat that mourning rage generates. Not to suppress it, but to help your system metabolize it without burning out your adrenals or keeping you awake at night. Bupleurum, a root used in traditional Chinese medicine, is specific for what's called "liver qi stagnation," the stuck, irritable, can't-quite-express-it feeling that characterizes this phase of grief. [Planetary Herbals Bupleurum Liver Cleanse (150 tabs)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00012TP3K?tag=spankyspinola-20) combines this root with other herbs that support the smooth flow of emotional energy without sedating you. In our experience, people notice they can feel their anger without it consuming them, like it has more room to breathe. Holy basil, or tulsi, works differently. It's an adaptogen that specifically supports the body's response to emotional stress. [Source Naturals Theanine Serene with Holy Basil](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0014H3B3G?tag=spankyspinola-20) pairs it with L-theanine, an amino acid that takes the sharp edges off anxiety without dulling your awareness. This can be helpful when the rage is keeping you up at night or making you feel like you might snap at any moment. You can read more about our approach to plant allies in [our herbal cabinet](/herbs), where we talk about matching remedy to the specific shape of what you're feeling. ## When Anger Meets the Body: Somatic Pathways Pat Ogden's work in sensorimotor psychotherapy emphasizes that trauma, and grief is a form of trauma, lives in procedural memory, the how-to memory of the body, not just in narrative memory. You can talk about your loss until you're blue in the face and still feel that coiled tension in your chest because the story and the sensation are stored in different systems. This is why movement practices often access grief that talk therapy can't reach. When you get on the floor and push against a wall with your whole body, when you twist a towel with everything you've got, when you beat a pillow or knead bread dough with focused intensity, you're speaking the language your nervous system understands. You're saying: yes, I feel this, I'm letting it move. The second year is an ideal time for this work because you're no longer in acute crisis. You have enough stability to resource yourself, to pause if it gets too intense, to integrate what comes up. The early days of loss often require just getting through. Now you can do the deeper metabolizing. One client described it to me this way: "It's like I've been carrying a backpack full of rocks and I kept trying to organize them differently so they'd be more comfortable. Then I realized I could just put the backpack down for a minute and feel how tired my shoulders are." The anger isn't the problem. The chronic bracing against the anger is the problem. ## What Nobody Tells You About the Second Year The first year has a script. Stages of grief, everyone says, as if they're orderly. People expect you to be sad. They know how to respond to tears. But the second year's rage confuses people. They think you should be over it by now. They don't understand why you're angrier now than you were at the funeral. Here's what they're missing: you're not angrier. You're finally able to feel what was there all along. The shock has metabolized enough that the underlying fury can surface. This is actually a sign of healing, even though it doesn't feel like one. Across the articles we've published on this site, one theme repeats: the feeling you're allowed to have is the one that's actually here, not the one you think you should have. If you're furious at the person who died for leaving, that's real. If you're enraged at God or the universe or bad luck, that's real. If you're angry at yourself for not preventing it, even when you logically know you couldn't have, that's real. The work isn't to talk yourself out of these feelings. The work is to let them have their full dimension so they can eventually transform into something else. Anger that's acknowledged and moved through often becomes fierce tenderness. It becomes a capacity to care deeply without trying to control outcomes. It becomes the part of you that refuses to abandon yourself the way you feel abandoned by the loss. But you can't skip to that part. You have to let the rage have its season. ## The Fire Toolkit and Emotional Alchemy We've written extensively about working with the fire element in [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit), and mourning rage is absolutely a fire phenomenon. It's hot, consuming, changing. Fire destroys, yes, but it also purifies. It burns through what's no longer needed and leaves mineral-rich ash that feeds new growth. The alchemical approach to emotion, which you can explore more deeply at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), suggests that every feeling has intelligence. Anger knows something sadness doesn't. It knows what mattered. It knows what was violated. It knows where your boundaries are and what you won't accept. When you try to suppress mourning rage, you lose access to that intelligence. You become passive in your grief, resigned, flattened. When you work with it, when you give it movement and sound and acknowledgment, it becomes a kind of fuel. Not for bitterness or vengeance, but for the radical act of continuing to care in a world that includes loss. This doesn't mean indulging every angry impulse. It means developing what [The Language of Emotions - Karla McLaren](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1583949968?tag=spankyspinola-20) calls "emotional agility," the capacity to feel something fully without immediately acting on it or pushing it away. You can be furious and still choose how you respond. But you can only make that choice if you're willing to feel the fury first. ## Common Questions About Mourning Rage ### Is it normal to be angrier in the second year than I was right after the loss? Absolutely. The first year often operates under a kind of protective numbness. Your system can only metabolize so much intensity at once. As the acute shock wears off and you have more capacity, the anger that was buffered can finally surface. This is your body's timeline, not a calendar's, and it's actually a sign that you're moving through the grief rather than staying stuck in the early shock phase. ### How do I express anger without hurting people I care about? The key is finding physical outlets that let your nervous system complete the defensive response without directing it at a person. Vigorous movement, working with your voice through toning or singing loudly in the car, physical labor like chopping wood or scrubbing floors, these give the energy somewhere to go. When you've discharged some of the physical charge, you're much more able to communicate about what you're feeling without it coming out sideways as criticism or withdrawal. ### What if I'm afraid the anger will never stop once I start feeling it? This is one of the most common fears, and it's based on a misunderstanding of how emotions work. Feelings aren't infinite. They're waves. When you actually let yourself feel anger fully, in your body, it peaks and then it passes. What makes anger feel endless is when you keep interrupting it, feeling a little bit, getting scared, pushing it down, then having it surge up again. The cycle of resistance is what makes it chronic. Full feeling, paradoxically, is what allows it to complete and transform. ## Where Rage Takes You The second year of grief might bring you to your knees in a different way than the first year did. The first year broke your heart. The second year might break open your ideas about who you're supposed to be in the face of loss. The person who stays composed. The person who finds meaning quickly. The person who doesn't make others uncomfortable with their ongoing sorrow. Mourning rage doesn't care about any of that. It cares about the truth of what you've lost and what that loss continues to mean as the months stack up and the person is still gone and the world is still indifferent and you're still here, changed in ways you didn't choose. There's a strange gift in this, though it might take years to recognize it as such. The rage teaches you something about your own aliveness. You can't be this angry about something that didn't matter. The fury is proportional to the love. It's the yang to grief's yin, the heat that keeps the sorrow from becoming a cold, dead weight. When you let yourself feel it, when you give it movement and breath and sound, you're saying: this mattered, I matter, the depth of this connection was real and I refuse to minimize it just to make my healing look pretty. You might find, as you work with mourning rage in its second year, that it slowly transmutes into something both fiercer and more tender. A kind of clarity about what you will and won't accept. A capacity to be with other people's pain without trying to fix it because you've learned to be with your own. A bone-deep knowing that love doesn't end just because a life does, and that your anger is one of the ways that love keeps speaking. For more on our approach to difficult emotions and the practices that support them, visit [the about page](/about) where we talk about why this work matters and how we approach it. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on mourning rage: the year after, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
